reviews.why: Same metric (average check = avg spend per customer) and same mechanism (menu pricing + mix), but the SCOPE differs. "average_check" is the consolidated/company-wide Cheesecake Factory brand average check (e.g. ~$29.40, $30.54, $31.05, $31.79 fiscal-wide figures, plus all the comp-sales % growth disclosures). "north_italia_average_check" is scoped specifically to the North Italia brand and is even split by daypart (~$34.60 lunch, ~$44.40 dinner for fiscal 2024) — a different reporting entity and a different dollar level. CAKE operates multiple concepts (The Cheesecake Factory, North Italia, Flower Child / Other FRC), and segment/brand-level average check is a distinct driver from the flagship/consolidated average check. Merging them would collapse a brand-specific sub-metric into the company-wide one, losing the per-concept distinction. Scope test fails, so keep separate.

Same metric type (average weekly sales per restaurant) and same calculation mechanism, but DIFFERENT scope. A's evidence is The Cheesecake Factory flagship brand (~$239,679 per operating week); B's evidence is the Flower Child brand specifically (~$85,200/week). These are two different restaurant concepts under the same parent (CAKE) with very different per-unit economics. The brand-specific driver is intentionally scoped to Flower Child, so it is not the same object as the company/flagship weekly-sales metric. Scope condition is false -> DIFFERENT; keep separate.

Different object/metric and mechanism. A = absolute industry-wide sales level (exogenous category demand: "broader casual dining sales trends", "black box casual dining index declining 410 bps"). B = CAKE's relative market share within that industry ("capture market share... outperformance versus the broader casual dining industry"). Object: total industry sales vs one company's relative slice. Mechanism: category demand lifting all players vs zero-sum competitive share shift — they can move oppositely (industry falls while CAKE gains share). Shared tokens are just industry context, not the same metric.

Only the brand name "Flower Child" is shared; the deterministic suggester flagged this on token overlap (child, flower) alone. All three SAME checks fail. (1) Object/metric differs: A is the acquisition/integration event ("bringing it fully under our umbrella" — a corporate ownership action), while B is the comparable-sales metric ("Flower Child comparable sales increased approximately X%" — an operating performance number). (2) Scope differs: A is the one-time M&A/ownership deal; B is recurring quarter-over-quarter store-level sales performance across many filings/calls. (3) Mechanism differs: A's mechanism is M&A (adding a brand to the portfolio); B's mechanism is organic demand at existing Flower Child locations driving comp-sales growth. A shared brand name does not merge an ownership-event driver with an operating same-store-sales driver. Keep separate.

Same brand (Flower Child) but two different metrics with different time scopes and units. (a) average unit volume = AUV, the annual revenue run-rate per restaurant ("$4.4 million average unit volume", "average volumes for a fully mature box"). (b) average weekly sales = the per-week sales figure ("average weekly sales were $85,200"). AUV is an annual-scale metric; weekly sales is a weekly-scale metric. Test 1 (same object): fails — annual run-rate volume vs weekly sales. Test 2 (same scope): fails — annual vs weekly time window. Although weekly sales roughly rolls up to AUV (~$85k/wk x 52 ~ $4.4M/yr), they are distinct reported metrics with different units and periodicity, not the same driver. Keep separate.

Both drivers concern the same Flower Child restaurant brand at CAKE, but they measure two different objects/metrics. (1) Object differs: "average unit volume" (AUV) measures sales revenue per restaurant box (evidence: "$4.4 million average unit volume", "potential average volumes for a fully mature box"), while "margin" measures profitability percentage (evidence: "margin target... mid-teens level"). AUV is a top-line volume metric; margin is a bottom-line profitability ratio. (2) Mechanism differs: AUV is driven by per-store sales throughput; margin is driven by cost structure relative to sales. These are not the same object or metric, so SAME is rejected on criteria 1 and 3. Token overlap on "flower"/"child" only reflects the shared brand, not a shared metric. Note: flower_child_margin already has same_as_variant "flower_child_restaurant_margin" (consistent margin family), distinct from the AUV family. Keep separate.

Same metric type (average unit volume / AUV) and same parent company (CAKE), but the SCOPE differs: these measure two distinct restaurant brands. Side A's evidence is about Flower Child ("flower child... potential average volumes for a fully mature box," ~$4.4M AUV). Side B's evidence is about North Italia ("North Italia restaurants were approximately $8.0 million," "North Italia... annualized AUVs of $7.7 million"). The token overlap (average/unit/volume) only reflects a shared metric name, not a shared object — Flower Child AUV and North Italia AUV are separate business drivers with materially different values ($4.4M vs $7.7-8.0M). Test fails on criterion 2 (same scope): different underlying brand/object. Keep separate.

Token overlap on "flower"/"child" only flags that both concern the same brand (Flower Child, a CAKE restaurant concept) — not the same driver. The three tests all fail: (1) Different object/metric: A = "average weekly sales" (a dollar-volume sales metric, $85,200, +6% YoY), B = "integration" (the operational/strategic effort of integrating the acquired brand). A sales number and an integration program are not the same object. (2) Different scope: A is a per-unit revenue performance measure; B is a corporate integration initiative. (3) Different mechanism: A reflects consumer demand/store productivity; B reflects acquisition assimilation work (systems, ops, teams). They share only the brand name, not the metric, scope, or mechanism. Keep separate — no SAME_AS.

Same brand (Flower Child, CAKE) but different metric and mechanism. (a) average weekly sales is a top-line revenue/volume measure in dollars ("$85,200, up more than 6%"). (b) margin is a profitability ratio ("margin target...mid-teens level"). Test 1 (same object/metric) FAILS: sales volume vs profit margin. Test 3 (same mechanism) FAILS: revenue throughput vs cost-to-revenue efficiency. Only scope (the Flower Child brand) matches. The token overlap on "child"/"flower" reflects the shared brand name, not a shared metric — a brand-collision false positive. Keep separate.

Different metric object, fails check #1 (same object/metric). Side A = "average weekly sales" (AWS): a dollar volume per restaurant per week — evidence quote: "Flower Child average weekly sales were $85,200, up more than 6%." This is an absolute productivity figure (dollars per unit time). Side B = "comparable/same-store sales" (comps): a growth percentage measuring sales change at restaurants open long enough to compare year-over-year — every B quote reads "comparable sales increased approximately X%." AWS measures dollar throughput of the average unit; comps measure period-over-period percentage growth on a stable store base. They are distinct restaurant KPIs that move independently (a chain can grow comps while AWS shifts differently due to unit mix/new openings). Token overlap (flower, child, sales) is superficial; the underlying measured object differs. Keep separate.

Same brand (Flower Child, a CAKE concept) but different metrics. A = average weekly sales = dollars of revenue per restaurant per week ("$85,200, up more than 6%"). B = unit count = number of locations operated ("38 Flower Child locations... ~20% annual unit growth"). Object differs (money/sales intensity vs store count), scope differs (per-unit sales rate vs total footprint), mechanism differs (sales productivity vs store expansion). The token overlap is only the brand name, which does not imply the same metric. All three SAME conditions fail, so keep separate.

Both mention the Flower Child brand, but they track different objects/mechanisms. "flower_child_integration" (2023 quote) is about the operational integration path of the Flower Child acquisition/business. "flower_child_rewards_program" (2025 quote) is about an app-based customer loyalty/points rewards program. Same brand, but different metric, different scope, and different mechanism. Token overlap on "flower"/"child" only reflects the shared brand name, not a shared driver. Fails criteria 1 and 3, so keep separate.

Both mention the "Flower Child" brand at CAKE, but they track different objects with different mechanisms. (1) Object/metric differs: "flower_child_integration" is about the operational integration/onboarding of the Flower Child acquisition ("we continue down the integration path"), while "flower_child_sales" is a revenue figure (e.g., "sales increased 11.0% to $127.5 million"). (2) Mechanism differs: integration = absorbing/incorporating an acquired business into operations; sales = top-line revenue generation from the brand. Integration progress and sales dollars are not the same driver. Token overlap on "flower"/"child" only reflects the shared brand name, not a shared metric or mechanism. Since object and mechanism both fail, keep separate.

Same brand (Flower Child), but the two drivers track different objects/mechanisms. (1) Object/metric differs: "flower_child_integration" is about the integration/onboarding process of the acquired Flower Child concept ("we continue down the integration path"); "flower_child_unit_count" is about the count and growth of physical store locations ("operate 38 Flower Child locations... 20% average annual unit growth"). Integration progress and store-count growth are distinct metrics. (2) Mechanism differs: integration = merging an acquired business into the company's operations; unit count = opening new restaurant locations. (3) The token overlap that triggered this candidate is only the shared brand name "flower_child," not a shared metric. Fails criteria 1 and 3, so keep separate.

Same business unit (Flower Child restaurant brand at CAKE), but the METRIC differs, so they are not the same driver.  - A = flower_child_margin: tracks the PROFIT MARGIN trajectory. Evidence: "margin target... mid-teens level... make it a growth vehicle." This is a profitability/margin object. - B = flower_child_sales: tracks SALES/REVENUE dollars and growth. All 7 evidence quotes are revenue figures: "sales increased 11.0% to $127.5 million," "sales totaled $32.2 million," "sales per operating week were $80,000."  Test 1 (same object/metric): FALSE. Margin (profitability ratio) is a different metric than sales (top-line revenue). A company can grow sales while margin lags, or improve margin while sales are flat. They are distinct, separately-tracked financials.  Tests 2 and 3 (scope, mechanism): scope overlaps (both = Flower Child unit), but mechanism differs — margin moves on cost/efficiency/pricing; sales move on volume/traffic/new-unit growth.  The token overlap ("flower," "child") only reflects the shared brand, not a shared metric. Keep separate.

Token overlap is just the shared brand name "Flower Child" (a CAKE restaurant concept), not a shared driver. The two refer to different objects/metrics. (a) "off-premise mix" = the share of sales that are takeout/delivery vs dine-in (55% off-premise). (b) "rewards program" = an app-based loyalty/points program. Fails all three tests: different object/metric (sales channel mix vs loyalty program), different scope, different mechanism (where food is consumed vs customer retention via points). Keep separate.

Token overlap on "flower"/"child" only means both are about the Flower Child brand — it does not mean the same driver. Test fails on object/metric and mechanism:  1. OBJECT/METRIC — DIFFERENT.    - A (off_premise_mix): the share of sales done off-premise (takeout/delivery), e.g. "you're talking about a 55% off-premise mix." This is a channel-mix percentage.    - B (same_store_sales): comparable-store sales growth, e.g. "Flower Child comparable sales increased approximately 6%." This is a year-over-year same-store revenue growth rate.    These are two distinct metrics. A 55% off-premise share and a 6% comp-sales increase measure completely different things.  2. SCOPE — both are the Flower Child concept (same brand, same company CAKE), so scope alone overlaps. But shared scope is not enough.  3. MECHANISM — DIFFERENT.    - A is driven by how customers order (dine-in vs takeout/delivery channel behavior).    - B is driven by traffic + check growth at existing stores vs prior year (demand strength / comp performance).    Off-premise mix can shift without comp sales changing, and comp sales can rise or fall regardless of the off-premise share.  The only evidence the two share is the same transcript date (2024-05-08), but the quotes pulled from it describe different things (one about off-premise %, one about comp-sales strength). Same source, different drivers.  Because object/metric and mechanism both fail, this is NOT a missed duplicate. Keep them separate — do NOT add a reversible SAME_AS link.

Token overlap flagged the shared brand name "Flower Child," but the two drivers are different objects. A (flower_child_rewards_program) is a customer loyalty initiative: "flower child is much more of a traditional rewards program it's an app based program that has points." B (flower_child_sales) is the brand's total revenue: "Flower Child sales increased 11.0% to $127.5 million." Fails all three tests: (1) different object/metric — a loyalty program vs. total dollar sales; (2) different scope — one feature/initiative vs. whole-brand top-line revenue; (3) different mechanism — customer-loyalty driver vs. revenue performance. The rewards program is at most a sub-component that could influence sales, not the same thing. Keep separate.

Token overlap on "flower"/"child" only reflects the shared brand (Flower Child, a CAKE concept), not a shared driver. The two fail all three tests. Object/metric: A is a customer loyalty/rewards program ("app based program that has points"); B is a sales-performance metric (comparable / same-store sales growth, e.g. "comparable sales increased approximately 6%"). Different objects entirely. Scope: A is a marketing program offering; B is a financial KPI tracking period-over-period sales at existing stores. Mechanism: A drives engagement/retention via points/app; B measures revenue performance of the concept's locations. Per the brand=own-driver rule, sharing the "Flower Child" brand does not make two distinct attributes the same driver. Keep separate.

Token overlap on "rewards"/"program" only — the evidence shows two different objects. (A) flower_child_rewards_program is the specific app-based, points-based loyalty program for ONE brand, Flower Child ("flower child is much more of a traditional rewards program it's an app based program that has points"). It is a description of a brand's loyalty product. (B) rewards_program_contribution is an analyst question about the financial CONTRIBUTION of loyalty — "about 100 basis points from a contribution perspective... from loyalty" — i.e., the margin/financial impact metric of the rewards program, not a specific brand's app. Fails all three SAME tests: (1) different object/metric — a loyalty product vs. a basis-point contribution figure; (2) different scope — one brand (Flower Child) vs. company-wide loyalty contribution; (3) different mechanism — describing how a program works vs. quantifying its financial impact. Keep separate.

These are two distinct metrics for the same brand (Flower Child), failing the "same object/metric" test.  SCOPE DIFFERS — the key distinction: - "flower_child_sales" = TOTAL sales (all locations). Evidence: "Flower Child sales increased 11.0% to $127.5 million for fiscal 2023" — absolute dollar figures across the entire concept, including new units. - "flower_child_same_store_sales" = COMPARABLE sales (a same-store, like-for-like base only). Evidence: "Flower Child comparable sales increased approximately 6%" — restricted to the comparable-store base, excluding new locations.  MECHANISM/OBJECT DIFFERS: - Total sales grows from BOTH new unit openings AND existing-store growth (e.g., +11.0% total). - Comparable sales isolates ONLY existing-store growth, removing the new-unit effect (e.g., +6% comp for the same period). These move differently and answer different questions.  This is the classic total-revenue vs. same-store-sales distinction in restaurant/retail reporting. They are deliberately separate line items in CAKE's own filings: the 10-Ks report "Flower Child sales" (total dollars) AND "Flower Child comparable sales" (comp %) as different figures for the same period. Merging them would conflate a top-line revenue total with a unit-economics comp metric.  Verdict DIFFERENT — keep separate. Rule 1 (same metric) FALSE and Rule 2 (same scope) FALSE.

These measure same-store (comparable) sales for two DIFFERENT restaurant brands that CAKE (Cheesecake Factory) owns: Flower Child vs North Italia. The token overlap that flagged them (sales, same, store) is only the shared metric TYPE ("comparable sales"), not the object. Test 1 (same object) fails: every Flower Child quote says "Flower Child" and every North Italia quote says "North Italia" — the evidence never mixes them. They are distinct segments with diverging numbers (e.g. fiscal 2024 10-K dated 2026-02-23: Flower Child +5% vs North Italia -2%; 2025-05-05 10-Q: Flower Child +5% vs North Italia -1%). Same metric type and same parent company, but different brand/scope. Keep separate.

Same metric type (comparable/same-store sales) and same mechanism (existing-store sales growth), but SCOPE differs. "flower_child_same_store_sales" is scoped to ONE concept/brand: Flower Child (every quote says "Flower Child comparable sales"). "same_store_sales" is the company-wide / Cheesecake Factory-concept comparable restaurant sales (quotes say "Comparable restaurant sales at The Cheesecake Factory restaurants," "an increase in comparable restaurant sales" driving total revenue, plus the fiscal.ai KPI "The Cheesecake Factory Restaurants Comparable Restaurant Sales"). CAKE reports Flower Child comps separately from its core/Cheesecake Factory comps (Flower Child even outpaces the rest of the system). Merging them would collapse a single-brand driver into the consolidated/core comp metric — different scope means a real over-merge. The token overlap (same/store/sales) is the suggester's hint only; meaning shows distinct objects. Keep separate.

These fail the same-scope test. (A) "international_same_store_sales" tracks comparable sales across The Cheesecake Factory's overseas/international locations worldwide ("all those different geographies... everywhere around the world"). (B) "north_italia_same_store_sales" tracks comparable restaurant sales for one specific U.S. brand, North Italia, an Italian-concept restaurant chain CAKE owns domestically. Same object type (same-store/comparable sales metric) and same mechanism (year-over-year comp sales), but scope differs: international geographic footprint vs. a single named domestic brand. North Italia is a domestic concept, not an international segment. A company can have flat international comps while North Italia declines (the 2025 evidence shows North Italia comps going negative). Merging would wrongly collapse a brand-level driver into a geographic one. Keep separate.

Token overlap (sales/same/store) suggested this, but evidence shows two of the three SAME tests fail. SCOPE differs: A is explicitly international ("all those different geographies," "everywhere around the world," comps positive across geographies), while B is company-wide consolidated comp (the headline 1%-2% range, implied 2026 number, Q2 2025 ~+1% total-company figures) — not international-only. MECHANISM differs: A is realized/actual same-store performance in international markets (an outcome), while B is forward-looking guidance/forecast of comp ("comp assumptions," "expect to be in that 1% to 2% range," "implied 2026 same-store sales number," "high end of our guide") — an expectations/guidance driver, not a performance one. Same broad metric family (comps) but different scope and different mechanism, so keep separate.

Same metric (menu mix % effect on average check) and same mechanism (customers shifting what they order), but SCOPE differs. menu_mix is CAKE's consolidated/company-wide menu mix used to explain total average-check variation across all filings ("a 1.9% negative change from menu mix", "Average check variations are driven by... changes in menu mix"). north_italia_menu_mix is scoped to ONE brand/concept — North Italia ("The north Italia mix was negative 2%") — which is a sub-segment of CAKE's portfolio, tracked and reported separately on earnings calls. The brand-level number can and does move independently of the total-company figure, so they are distinct drivers. Scope test fails (whole company vs single brand) -> keep separate to avoid collapsing a brand-specific sub-driver into the company-wide driver.

Token overlap on "new" and "restaurant" only — the underlying meaning is opposite. Test fails on all three checks:  1. Object/metric — DIFFERENT. `new_restaurant_capex` = capital expenditures (dollars SPENT building new locations: "$76.1M and $61.6M for the first nine months"). `new_restaurant_margin` = profitability of new restaurants once open ("bring the early stages of the restaurants up faster... mature margin"). One is cash invested to build; the other is profit margin earned after opening.  2. Scope — DIFFERENT. Capex covers locations still "under development" (pre-opening construction spend). Margin covers restaurants already operating and ramping toward maturity (post-opening performance).  3. Mechanism — DIFFERENT. Capex moves with the pace/cost of building stores (investment cash outflow). Margin moves with how fast a newly opened store reaches mature unit economics (operating profitability). No shared driver.  Same company (CAKE) and same general topic (new restaurants), but spend-to-build vs profit-after-open are distinct, even opposing, financial concepts. Keep separate.

Different object, mechanism, and cost type — they only share the tokens "cost" and "restaurant". Side A (new_restaurant_food_cost_efficiency) is about OPERATING costs at new units: food cost and labor productivity inefficiencies that hurt restaurant-level margins until a new store ramps to normal traffic ("actual-to-theoretical food cost inefficiencies and labor productivity inefficiencies"). Side B (restaurant_construction_cost) is about CAPITAL/BUILD-OUT costs to physically construct a restaurant — construction labor unionization, raw materials, and ~$1,100–$1,200 per square foot to build the premises. Object differs (food/labor margin efficiency vs. construction build-out spend). Mechanism differs (ramp-up operational margin drag vs. capex to open a unit). Even the shared "new restaurants" scope describes different things: A = post-opening operating margins, B = pre-opening physical construction. All three SAME tests fail, so keep them separate.

Same brand scope (North Italia) but different metric and mechanism. A = "average check" = total dollars spent per customer visit ($34.60 lunch / $44.40 dinner), an absolute dollar-per-ticket level. B = "menu mix" ("the mix was negative 2%") = the percentage contribution to same-store sales from customers shifting toward higher- or lower-priced items, one component of comp-sales growth. Test 1 (same object/metric) and Test 3 (same mechanism) both fail; only the shared brand name overlaps. Keep separate.

Same brand (North Italia / CAKE) but two unrelated metrics: A measures average check (dollars spent per customer per visit, ~$34.60 lunch / $44.40 dinner); B measures restaurant count (number of company-owned locations). Fails object/metric (spend-per-customer vs store count) and mechanism (menu pricing/mix vs openings/closings). Token overlap is only the shared brand name, not a shared measure. Keep separate.

Same brand (North Italia) but two different financial objects. (1) Object/metric: A = average check (dollar revenue per customer per visit, ~$34.60 lunch / $44.40 dinner); B = restaurant-level (four-wall) margin (a profitability percentage, 13.3%-15%). Revenue-per-ticket is not the same metric as a profit margin. (2) Scope: A measures per-customer spend across visits; B measures location-level operating profitability. (3) Mechanism: A is driven by menu mix, pricing, and party spend; B is driven by margin = revenue minus restaurant operating costs (labor, food, occupancy). All three checks fail, so keep them separate.

These are two distinct metrics for the same brand (North Italia), not the same metric. (1) Same object/metric? NO. "Average check" measures the dollar amount one customer spends per visit (evidence: "average check... averaged approximately $34.60 for lunch and $44.40 for dinner"). "Same-store / comparable sales" measures the percent change in total revenue at established restaurants vs a prior period (evidence: "comparable sales increased approximately 8% from fiscal 2022"). Different objects: a per-customer dollar level vs a year-over-year revenue growth rate. (2) Same mechanism? NO. Average check moves with menu pricing and items per order; comparable sales moves with traffic plus check, measured as a YoY delta. A store can have a rising average check while comparable sales fall (or vice versa). They are related inputs but not the same measurement. The token overlap ("north", "italia") only reflects the shared brand, not a shared metric. Keep separate.

These are two distinct restaurant metrics that share the brand name "North Italia" but measure different things. (1) Object/metric: FAILS. "average_check" = dollar amount spent per customer per visit ($34.60 lunch, $44.40 dinner per the 10-K). "traffic" = number of guests/visit count (transcripts cite "traffic was negative 4%", "negative 6%"). Different objects entirely. (2) Scope: both are North Italia brand, but the transcripts explicitly separate the components — price, traffic, and mix are reported as distinct line items ("Price is between 4.5% and 5%. Traffic was negative 4%"). Average check is the price/spend dimension; traffic is the volume dimension. (3) Mechanism: FAILS. Average check moves with menu pricing and mix (what each guest spends); traffic moves with guest count (how many guests come in). In restaurant unit economics these are deliberately decomposed because sales = traffic x average check — they are multiplicative inputs, not the same driver. The transcript even names them side by side as separate components. Approving SAME would re-merge two orthogonal demand inputs, exactly the kind of over-merge to avoid. Note: "north_italia_traffic" correctly already carries same_as_variant "north_italia_restaurant_traffic" (a true synonym), which confirms the traffic node's identity is volume — not check size. Verdict: keep separate.

These are two distinct financial metrics for the same brand (North Italia), not the same metric. Driver A = Average Unit Volume (AUV): a SALES/REVENUE measure stated in dollars per location (e.g. "$8.0 million", "annualized AUVs of $7.7 million"). Driver B = restaurant-level / four-wall MARGIN: a PROFITABILITY ratio stated as a percentage (e.g. "13.3%", "restaurant-level margin... was 15%"). Test 1 (same object/metric): FALSE — revenue-per-store vs profit-margin-percent are different objects with different units (dollars vs %). Test 3 (same mechanism): FALSE — AUV is driven by traffic/check/store volume; margin is driven by cost structure relative to sales (food/labor/menu pricing). The token overlap ("north", "italia") only reflects the shared brand, not the metric. Keep separate.

Only the brand (North Italia) is shared — that's why token overlap flagged it. The metrics differ: A = "food costs" (an expense / cost-of-ingredients line, mechanism = deploying best practices to LOWER input cost). B = "north Italia mix was negative 2%" (a sales-mix / comparable-sales composition metric on the revenue side). Test 1 (same object/metric) fails: cost line vs sales-mix percentage. Test 3 (same mechanism) fails: cost reduction vs revenue-mix shift. Same brand scope is not enough; these are distinct drivers. Keep separate.

Token overlap (italia, north) only flagged these because both concern the North Italia brand — but they measure entirely different things. Side A (north_italia_food_cost) is about lowering FOOD COSTS over time via deployed best practices — an expense/margin metric (cost of goods). Side B (north_italia_same_store_sales) is comparable/same-store SALES growth — a revenue/demand metric, evidenced by ~20 quotes all reporting comparable sales percentage changes. Test 1 (same object/metric): FALSE — food cost (an input expense) vs comparable sales (a revenue/demand output) are distinct objects. Test 3 (same mechanism): FALSE — cost reduction via operational best practices vs sales growth via customer demand. Only the scope (North Italia brand) overlaps. Two of three checks fail, so keep them separate.

Same restaurant brand (North Italia, a CAKE concept), but different metric, scope, and mechanism. (A) food_cost = cost-of-goods / margin metric; evidence is about deploying best practices to "lower food costs over time" (input cost side). (B) traffic = guest count / footfall demand metric; evidence quotes "Traffic was negative 4%/-6%" (top-line demand side). Object differs (cost % vs guest count), and mechanism differs (supply-chain/operational cost control vs consumer demand). Token overlap on "italia/north" only reflects the shared brand, not the same driver. Fails criteria 1 and 3 → keep separate. Note: the variant "north_italia_restaurant_traffic" on side B is a true synonym of traffic, but that belongs to B, not to A.

Same company (CAKE) and same brand (North Italia), but these measure two different things. (A) menu_mix evidence: "The north Italia mix was negative 2%" — this is mix, the shift in what guests order (cheaper vs pricier items), measured as a negative percentage. (B) menu_pricing evidence: "we have another price increase in October... for North Italia" and "the pricing you just took, were you behind on pricing" — this is menu price increases the company sets. Fails check 1 (different metric: mix shift vs price increase) and check 3 (different mechanism: guest ordering behavior vs management raising prices). Mix and pricing are standard, distinct components of restaurant same-store-sales (pricing + mix + traffic). Keep separate.

These are two distinct metrics for North Italia, sharing only the brand name (token overlap "north, italia" is why they were flagged). Fails all three SAME tests. (1) Same object/metric: NO. "A" tracks menu mix — the sales-mix shift, quoted at "negative 2%." "B" tracks restaurant-level (four-wall) margin — a profitability percentage, quoted at 13.3% and 15%. Mix shift and margin are different objects. (2) Same scope: partly overlaps (both North Italia brand) but the metric scope differs — A is a top-line sales composition metric, B is a unit-economics profit metric. (3) Same mechanism: NO. Menu mix reflects what customers order (product mix shifting sales up/down). Restaurant-level margin reflects cost structure and pricing flowing to four-wall profit. Note B's own same_as_variants (four_wall_margin, restaurant_margin) are all margin synonyms — none mention mix, confirming the margin lane is separate from the mix lane. Keep separate; do not add SAME_AS.

Same brand (North Italia, a CAKE restaurant concept) but DIFFERENT metric and mechanism. (a) "north_italia_menu_mix" measures menu mix shift — quote: "The north Italia mix was negative 2%" — this is a sales/menu-mix metric tracking how product mix affects sales, expressed as a percentage change in mix. (b) "north_italia_segment_margin" measures segment profitability — quote: "the North Italia segment margins, did you say what the mature units were" — this is a margin/profitability metric. Test 1 (same object/metric): FALSE — menu mix is a sales-composition metric; segment margin is a profitability metric. Test 3 (same mechanism): FALSE — mix shift vs. operating margin are different causal mechanisms. The shared tokens "north" and "italia" only reflect the same brand, not the same driver. Brand match alone does not make these the same metric. Keep separate.

These are two distinct restaurant sales metrics for the same brand (North Italia, CAKE). The evidence quotes explicitly separate them: "mix was negative 2%... Traffic was negative 4%" and "traffic was negative 6%, prices about 4%, and the mix was negative 2%." Test 1 (same object/metric) FAILS: menu_mix measures the shift in sales toward higher/lower-priced items (product/check-mix effect), while traffic measures customer/guest counts (volume of visits). They are reported as separate line items alongside price, and they move independently (mix steady at -2% across quarters while traffic swung -4% to -6%). Test 3 (same mechanism) also FAILS: mix is driven by what guests order, traffic by how many guests come in. Same scope (North Italia brand) but different object and different mechanism, so the pair is DIFFERENT. Keep separate; do not add SAME_AS.

Token overlap on "north"+"italia" only flags the same brand (North Italia), not the same metric. The two fail all three SAME tests:  1. Object/metric — DIFFERENT. A measures menu price increases ("another price increase in October," "the pricing you just took"). B measures store footprint ("North Italia Company-Owned Restaurants" = a count of locations). Price ≠ unit count.  2. Scope — DIFFERENT. A is about pricing actions on the menu. B is the number of company-owned restaurant units.  3. Mechanism — DIFFERENT. A moves via menu price changes (pricing lever). B moves via opening/closing locations (unit growth lever).  Same brand, two unrelated drivers. Keep separate.

Token overlap (italia, north) is just the brand name, not the metric. The two drivers measure different objects/metrics with different mechanisms:  Side A (north_italia_menu_pricing): The OBJECT is the PRICE the company SETS. Evidence is about taking a "price increase in October" and being "behind on pricing" — i.e., the company's own pricing actions/decisions (a lever the company pulls).  Side B (north_italia_same_store_sales): The OBJECT is comparable/same-store SALES — a demand/performance OUTCOME ("comparable sales increased approximately 8% from fiscal 2022"). This measures how much customers spent vs. prior year, an output.  Test results: 1. Same object/metric? NO. Menu pricing = price level the company sets. Same-store sales = realized comparable revenue growth. These are different metrics. 2. Same scope? Both are North Italia brand-level, so scope matches. 3. Same mechanism? NO. Pricing is a company-controlled input/lever; same-store sales is a market outcome driven by traffic + pricing + mix. Pricing is one of several inputs INTO same-store sales, not the same thing.  Because object/metric and mechanism differ, these are distinct drivers. Keep separate.

Only the brand name "North Italia" is shared — that is the token overlap (italia, north) the suggester flagged, not a real match. Test 1 (same object/metric) FAILS: "a" is a COUNT of company-owned restaurant locations (a unit count), while "b" is a segment profit MARGIN (a profitability percentage) — different objects entirely. Test 3 (same mechanism) FAILS: counting open units vs. computing profitability of a business segment are different mechanisms. Test 2 (scope = North Italia brand) is the only one that holds. Since tests 1 and 3 are false, verdict is DIFFERENT — keep them separate, no SAME_AS.

Two different North Italia margin metrics, not the same thing. (1) Different object/metric: side A is "restaurant-level"/"four-wall" margin (revenue minus store-level operating costs), confirmed by its quotes ("Forewall margin... 13.3%", "restaurant-level margin... 15%") and its variants (four_wall, restaurant). Side B is "segment margin" — a reporting-segment profitability figure that layers in more costs. In restaurant reporting these are recognized as distinct metrics. (2) Different scope: side A is explicitly the "adjusted mature North Italia locations" (a subset), while side B's quote is "the North Italia segment margins" with the analyst asking about "the mature units within that" — treating mature units as a slice INSIDE the broader segment. So segment (whole) vs mature locations (subset). (3) Different mechanism for the same reason — four-wall/store-level economics vs segment-level economics include different cost layers. The match was suggested only by token overlap (italia, margin, north); the underlying metric and scope differ. Fails object and scope tests, so keep separate.

Token overlap on "north"/"italia" only flags the same brand (North Italia), not the same metric. The two drivers measure different objects: (A) restaurant-level / four-wall margin — a profitability percentage (13.3%, 15%) driven by menu price and cost structure; (B) traffic — a guest-count volume measure (negative 4%, negative 6%) shown alongside price and mix. Fails test 1 (different object/metric: margin % vs traffic count) and test 3 (different mechanism: cost-and-price profitability vs customer-volume change). Same brand and company, but margin and traffic are distinct operating metrics. Keep separate.

Same mechanism and metric type (comparable/same-store restaurant sales), but DIFFERENT scope. Side A is exclusively North Italia comparable sales — every one of its ~20 evidence quotes is scoped to the North Italia brand (e.g. "North Italia comparable sales increased approximately 8% from fiscal 2022"). Side B is The Cheesecake Factory restaurants / company-wide comparable restaurant sales — its quotes are scoped to "The Cheesecake Factory restaurants" or the consolidated company (e.g. "Comparable restaurant sales at The Cheesecake Factory restaurants increased 4.0%"; revenue-driver language; fiscal.ai KPI = "The Cheesecake Factory Restaurants Comparable Restaurant Sales"). North Italia is one separately-reported brand within CAKE's portfolio; its same-store-sales is a distinct, separately-disclosed object from the Cheesecake-Factory/company-level same-store-sales. Scope test fails (brand-specific vs company/Cheesecake-Factory-specific), so keep separate. Token overlap (sales/same/store) is the only similarity. Note: A's same_as_variant "north_italia_comparable_sales" is correctly a same-brand variant, reinforcing that A is the North Italia roll-up, not the general metric.

Only the brand "North Italia" is shared; the metrics differ. A = segment profit margin (a profitability metric: how much money the segment keeps), evidenced by "the North Italia segment margins." B = guest traffic (a demand/volume metric: how many customers visit), evidenced by "Traffic was negative 4%/negative 6%." Fails test 1 (different object/metric: margin vs traffic count) and test 3 (different mechanism: cost/revenue profitability vs customer foot traffic). Margin and traffic can move in opposite directions, so they are independent drivers. The token overlap (italia, north) only reflects the common brand name, not the same metric. Keep separate.

Token overlap only ("supply", "chain") — opposite meanings on all three tests. supply_chain_disruption = NEGATIVE risk: shortages, transportation delays, COVID/geopolitical-driven cost inflation, delayed restaurant openings (15+ evidence quotes: "supply shortages and transportation delays," "disruptions in the supply chain," "supply chain challenges"). supply_chain_integration = POSITIVE operational improvement: one transcript quote "Made some really strong improvements there on supply chain integration." Object differs (external disruption event vs internal integration capability), scope differs (cost/availability headwind vs operational efficiency initiative), mechanism differs (supply breakdown raising costs/delays vs management consolidating/optimizing the supply chain). Merging would fuse a risk-driver with an improvement-driver. Keep separate.

Both mention CAKE's "supply chain" (shared object, shared company-wide scope), but the mechanisms differ. (a) integration = operational improvement in connecting/streamlining supply chain operations ("strong improvements on supply chain integration"). (b) scale = volume/size-driven leverage and purchasing power as the company grows ("leverage our scalable supply chain", "benefit on the increasing scale of our company", cross-brand purchasing into North Italia). A firm can improve integration without growing scale, and gain scale benefits without improving integration. Mechanism test fails; the token overlap (supply, chain) is a surface match only. Keep separate.
